feedback.text: As a concerned citizen and supporter of the European Citizens' Initiative Stop Killing Games, I welcome the European Commissions efforts to tackle unfair digital practices through the Digital Fairness Act. However, I urge you to explicitly include unfair commercial practices in the video game industry within the scope of this legislation. The Stop Killing Games initiativenow supported by over one million EU citizenshighlights a growing problem: many game publishers deliberately shut down online games that consumers have purchased, rendering them unusable. This practice exploits the imbalance between publishers and consumers and undermines the principle of digital ownership. Key concerns include: Deceptive marketing: Games are sold without clear disclosure that they may be made unplayable in the near future. Lack of redress: Consumers are left with no compensation or alternatives when a game is taken offline. Erosion of digital rights: Users are effectively renting products advertised as purchases. We believe that: Publishers should not be allowed to disable or remove access to games that consumers have bought. Consumers should have the right to retain access to purchased digital content, especially for single-player or peer-hosted games. The Digital Fairness Act must recognize these practices as unfair, misleading, and exploitative. Digital fairness means protecting consumers in all digital marketsincluding the video game sector, which affects millions of EU citizens, especially younger generations. We call on the Commission to listen to the voices of European citizens and ensure that the final text of the Digital Fairness Act reflects this growing concern. This is a critical opportunity to build a more just, transparent, and consumer-friendly digital future in Europe.
